content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array a1,…,ana_1,\ldots, a_na1​,…,an​ of nnn elements. He wants you to answer queries of the following type:

Compute f(L,R)f(L, R)f(L,R), where f(L,R)=∑i=LR∑j=iRgcd⁡(ai,ai+1,…aj).f(L, R) = \sum_{i = L}^{R} \sum_{j= i}^{R} \gcd(a_i, a_{i+1}, \dots a_j).f(L,R)=∑i=LR​∑j=iR​gcd(ai​,ai+1​,…aj​). In other words, we want to compute the sum of the greatest common divisors over all subarrays of some range [L,R][L, R][L,R].
Help Chef answer qqq queries of the described type.

###Input:

The first line contains two integers nnn and qqq - the size of the array and the number of queries.

The second line contains nnn integers a1,…,ana_1,\ldots,a_na1​,…,an​.

Each of the following qqq lines contains two integers lll, rrr.

The queries are encrypted. Let the answer to the previous query be xxx (or 000 if there is none). The query is decrypted as follows: L=((l+x)mod  n)+1L=((l+x)\mod n)+1L=((l+x)modn)+1 and R=((r+x)mod  n)+1R=((r+x) \mod n)+1R=((r+x)modn)+1.

###Output:

Print qqq integers - the answer to each query modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.

###Constraints

1≤n≤2⋅1051 \leq n \le 2\cdot 10^51≤n≤2⋅105

1≤q≤1061 \leq q \le 10^61≤q≤106

1≤ai≤1091 \leq a_{i} \le 10^91≤ai​≤109

0≤l,r≤n0 \leq l, r \le n0≤l,r≤n
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T – the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers n and q – the size of the array and the number of queries.
- The second line contains n integers a1,…,an.
- Each of the next q lines contains two integers l and r – the encrypted query parameters.
The queries must be answered in the given order. Let x be the answer to the previous query (or 0 before the first query of a test case). The real parameters are
L = ((l + x) mod n) + 1,
R = ((r + x) mod n) + 1.
It is guaranteed that after decryption L ≤ R.
All answers have to be printed modulo 1 000 000 007.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output a single line containing f(L,R) modulo 1 000 000 007, in the order the queries appear.

solution.approach:
For every position i we keep the list of distinct GCD values of all sub‑arrays that end at i. While scanning the array from left to right we maintain a vector cur where each element is a pair (g, cnt) meaning that exactly cnt sub‑arrays ending at i have GCD = g. This vector can be built from the vector of the previous position by taking GCD(g, a[i]) for each pair and merging equal GCDs – the size of the vector is O(log A).
Each pair (g, cnt) corresponds to a contiguous segment of start positions: if the sub‑arrays ending at i with GCD = g start at positions [i‑cnt+1, i]. Therefore we can add g to a Fenwick tree (BIT) on the whole segment [i‑cnt+1, i] (range add, point query) or, equivalently, we use a BIT that supports range addition and prefix sum query. While iterating i from 1 to n we:
1. Build the new GCD list for i.
2. For every (g, cnt) in the list add g to the BIT on the range [i‑cnt+1, i].
3. Answer all queries whose right endpoint R equals i by querying the BIT for the sum on [L, i].
Because each position creates at most O(log A) segments, the total number of BIT updates is O(n log A). Each query is answered in O(log n). The whole algorithm works in O((n+q) log n) time and O(n log A) memory, easily fitting the limits.
The decryption of queries is performed on‑the‑fly using the answer of the previous query (initially 0).
solution.timeComplexity: O((n + q) · log n) – each update or query on the Fenwick tree costs O(log n). The number of GCD segments per position is O(log A) (A ≤ 10^9).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + q) for storing queries and the Fenwick tree, plus O(log A) temporary vectors per iteration.
